title: Osso buco alla milanese
subtitle: Braised veal shin with gremolata, served with saffron risotto
excerpt: Thick cross-sections of veal shin braised low and slow in white wine, stock, and vegetables until the meat is falling from the bone and the marrow is soft in the hollow centre. Finished with gremolata (a fresh mixture of lemon zest, garlic, and parsley) and traditionally served with risotto alla milanese.
sourceNotes: Osso buco alla milanese is documented in Milanese cookery literature from the nineteenth century. The dish is associated with the Lombard tradition of braised meats and is described by Pellegrino Artusi in La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene (1891, Italian public domain). The gremolata finish and the pairing with saffron risotto are the classic Milanese form.

content.text: Osso buco means hollow bone, and the marrow inside is part of the dish. Scoop it out with a small spoon at the table and spread it on bread or stir it into the risotto. The braise is built in two stages: the shin pieces are browned hard to build a crust, then the aromatics and liquid go in and everything cooks very gently for 1 hour 20 minutes to 1 hour 40 minutes, until the meat pulls away from the bone with no resistance.

The gremolata goes in at the end, not during cooking. It adds brightness that would be lost in the braise. Tie the pieces of shin with kitchen twine before browning if you want them to hold their shape through the cooking, this is worth doing for a dinner party, less important if you are eating at home.

items.name: Beef shin
items.prepNote: veal, cut into 4 cm thick cross-sections, 4 pieces

content.text: Season the {{beef-shin}} of veal shin on both sides with salt and pepper. Dust lightly with the {{plain-flour}} of flour, shaking off the excess. Heat the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large casserole over high heat. Brown the shin pieces two at a time for 3 to 4 minutes per side until deeply coloured. Remove and set aside.

Reduce the heat to medium. In the same pan, cook the {{onion}} onion, {{carrot}} carrots, and {{celery}} celery sticks for 10 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 minute. Pour in the {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine and cook, scraping the base of the pan, until reduced by half. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, {{stock-chicken}} of warm stock, {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves, and {{thyme}} of thyme. Return the shin pieces to the pan. The liquid should come about halfway up the sides of the meat.

Bring to a simmer, cover with a lid, and cook over very low heat for 1 hour 20 minutes to 1 hour 40 minutes, turning the shin pieces every 30 minutes, until the meat pulls away from the bone with no resistance. Remove the bay and thyme. Mix together the lemon zest, {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley, and the extra garlic clove to make the gremolata. Stir two thirds of the gremolata into the braise and scatter the rest over the top. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Braise at the lowest possible simmer; the liquid should barely move; check at 90 minutes and continue in 15-minute increments if needed
items.cause: Heat too high, or shin pieces too thick
items.symptom: Meat is tough after the stated cooking time

items.fix: Remove the lid for the last 20 minutes of cooking to reduce the liquid; or remove the meat when done and reduce the sauce separately over high heat
items.cause: Lid not on, or too much stock added

items.fix: Tie each piece with kitchen twine around the circumference before browning; remove the string before serving
items.symptom: Shin pieces have split and lost their shape

content.text: Osso buco is a Milanese dish, part of the same tradition as cotoletta and risotto alla milanese. The classic pairing is braised shin with saffron risotto. Stir the marrow into the risotto at the table and the dish lifts. The gremolata is what sets the Milanese version apart. Fresh lemon and garlic cut through the rich, long-cooked meat in a way no extra seasoning can.
